title: On the computability of optimal Scott sentences
overall_summary: The paper studies the gap between ordinary boldface Scott complexity and computable Scott complexity for computable countable structures. Its main construction gives a computable ∃-atomic structure, hence a structure with a Π2 Scott sentence, but with no computable Π3 Scott sentence; using a standard disjoint-union trick this yields a computable structure with a Π2 Scott sentence but no computable Σ4 Scott sentence. A second construction shows that, in any sufficiently rich language, the index set of computable structures with a computable Π2 Scott sentence is Π1^1-m-complete, so there is no low-complexity effective characterization of when the optimal boldface Scott sentence can be made computable. The core techniques are c.e. labelled bouquet/flower graphs, sorts devoted to diagonalizing against individual computable infinitary sentences, stagewise-isomorphic direct limits A and B_e whose limits separate, and tree/path coding of well-foundedness. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the robust Scott-rank equivalence between low Scott complexity and existential orbit definitions, the examples separating existential/boldface definability from effective computable Scott data, and the labelled-graph/direct-limit methods for tightly controlling orbits while defeating effective infinitary definitions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 (Montalbán, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure in a countable language and let α be a countable ordinal. The following are equivalent: (1) A has a Π_{α+1} Scott sentence in L_{ω1ω}; (2) for every finite tuple ā from A, the automorphism orbit of ā in A is definable without parameters by a Σ_α formula, equivalently A has a Scott family consisting of Σ_α formulas; (3) in the standard Borel space Mod(L) of countable L-structures, the isomorphism class {B ∈ Mod(L) : B ≅ A} is a Π^0_{α+1} set; (4) A is boldface Δ^0_α-categorical, in Montalbán's sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem rather than reproving it. The underlying mechanism is the Scott analysis by back-and-forth relations: stabilization at level α gives formulas of complexity Σ_α defining finite-tuple automorphism orbits, and the conjunction of the orbit descriptions yields a Π_{α+1} Scott sentence. Conversely, a Π_{α+1} Scott sentence forces the relevant back-and-forth equivalence classes/orbits to be Σ_α-definable. The Borel and categoricity clauses are the descriptive-set-theoretic and relative-computability forms of the same back-and-forth analysis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Standard computable upper bound from the introduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable countable structure and let α be a computable ordinal. If A has a Π_α Scott sentence, then A has a computable Π_{2α} Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls the standard argument. If A has a Π_α Scott sentence, then for every countable structure B one has A ≤_α B if and only if A ≅ B, where ≤_α is the usual back-and-forth preorder at level α. For computable A and computable α, the definition of A ≤_α B can be written as a computable Π_{2α} infinitary sentence ψ in the language of B. Thus B ⊧ ψ iff A ≤_α B iff B ≅ A, so ψ is a computable Π_{2α} Scott sentence for A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 disjoint-union observation A·ω
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Given a countable structure A, let A·ω be the structure consisting of an equivalence relation E with infinitely many E-classes, each E-class carrying a copy of A and with no additional interaction between distinct classes. For any ordinal α: (1) if A has a Π_α Scott sentence, respectively a computable Π_α Scott sentence, then A·ω has a Π_α Scott sentence, respectively a computable Π_α Scott sentence; (2) if A has no Π_α Scott sentence, respectively no computable Π_α Scott sentence, then A·ω has no Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence, respectively no computable Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), one writes a sentence saying that E has infinitely many classes and that the structure induced on every E-class satisfies the given Scott sentence for A; the construction preserves computability of the infinitary sentence. For (2), the paper uses the standard fact, attributed to Montalbán, that a countable structure B has a Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence iff there is a finite tuple b from B such that the pointed structure (B,b) has a Π_α Scott sentence. Applying this to B = A·ω, a hypothetical Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence for A·ω would yield a Π_α Scott analysis of a finitely pointed disjoint union of copies of A, from which a Π_α Scott sentence for the repeated component A is obtained; contrapositively, if A lacks such a Π_α Scott sentence, then A·ω cannot have the Σ_{α+1} one. The same reasoning works in the computable setting.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Bouquet graph fact used repeatedly (Montalbán, Lemma 8.17 of [Mon21])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the bouquet graph G_1(F) associated with a collection F of subsets of ω, where elements are flower centers and labels are coded by loops of specified lengths, G_1(F) is ∃-atomic exactly when F is discrete. In the paper's applications, discreteness is witnessed by finite sets of labels distinguishing each flower type, up to intended duplicates.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this fact rather than proving it. Its use is straightforward: a label ℓ_n is realized by adding a loop of length n+3, so labels are c.e.-addable during a computable construction. If every eventual flower type has a finite set of labels not shared by any different flower type, then the corresponding orbit is isolated by an existential formula asserting the presence of those finitely many loops. Since ∃-atomic structures are precisely the structures with Π2 Scott sentences in the setting used here, the finite-distinguishing-label checks in the constructions yield Π2 Scott sentences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1 (Alvir-Knight-McCoy, reproved as a warmup)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable structure with a Π2 Scott sentence but with no computable Π2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper sketches a new proof using labelled bouquet graphs. Enumerate all computable Π2 sentences as θ_e = ∧_{i∈ω} ∀x_{e,i} φ_{e,i}(x_{e,i}), where the formulas φ_{e,i} are uniformly computable Σ1 formulas and the tuple arities are computable. Build a computable labelled structure A with sort labels u_e, one sort U_e for each requirement against θ_e, and labels ℓ_i and ℓ_i^†. Simultaneously, for each e, build a comparison structure B_e that differs from A only in sort U_e and is stagewise isomorphic to A. In sort e, A has active elements a_1,...,a_n and B_e has b_1,...,b_{n-1},c. A stage is e-expansionary if the current finite approximation satisfies the first k universal conjuncts of θ_e on the relevant finite domain. At each expansionary stage, the construction labels the current last A-element with a new ℓ_i^†, creates a new A-element, creates a corresponding b_i in B_e, and lets the special element c in B_e continue accumulating labels. If there are infinitely many expansionary stages, then A ⊧ θ_e, B_e ⊧ θ_e, but B_e is not isomorphic to A because c has infinitely many ℓ_i-labels while no element of A does. If there are only finitely many expansionary stages, then some finite universal conjunct of θ_e fails permanently in A, so A ⊭ θ_e. In every sort, each non-duplicate element is isolated by either an ℓ_i^† label or by a unique ℓ_i label, so the bouquet graph is ∃-atomic and hence has a Π2 Scott sentence. Therefore no computable Π2 sentence θ_e is a Scott sentence for A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, strengthened form proved in Section 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable structure with a Π2 Scott sentence but with no computable Σ4 Scott sentence. More specifically, Section 3 constructs a computable ∃-atomic structure A with a Π2 Scott sentence and no computable Π3 Scott sentence; applying the Section 2 A·ω observation to this A gives the stated no-computable-Σ4 example.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Enumerate all computable Π3 sentences as θ_e = ∧_{i∈ω} ∀x_{e,i} ∨_{j∈ω} ∃y_{e,i,j} φ_{e,i,j}(x_{e,i},y_{e,i,j}), where the φ_{e,i,j} are uniformly computable Π1 formulas and the tuple arities are computable. The structure A is a computable labelled bouquet graph. Sort labels u_e partition the domain into sorts U_e, and sort U_e is used to diagonalize against θ_e. For each e a comparison structure B_e is built, equal to A outside U_e. At every stage s, finite/current approximations A_s and B_{e,s} are isomorphic. Inside U_e, A_s has active elements a_1,...,a_n, possibly with duplicates sharing exactly the same labels, while B_{e,s} has b_1,...,b_{n-1},c, with c corresponding at that stage to the current last active A-element a_n. Requirements R^e_{i,b} are indexed by i and by tuples b from B_e of the arity of x_{e,i}; each requirement starts at a stage t when b is present, stores that t and a counter k, and aims to ensure that if the matching tuple a in A satisfies the inner Σ2 part ∨_j ∃y φ_{e,i,j}(a,y), then b does so in B_e. Equivalently, if b witnesses failure of θ_e in B_e by satisfying ∧_j ∀y ¬φ_{e,i,j}(b,y), the construction must make A fail θ_e too. Requirement R^e_{i,b} requires attention at stage s if B_{e,s} satisfies the finite approximation ∧_{j≤k} ∀y ∈ B_{e,t+k} ¬φ_{e,i,j}(b,y), with the quantified tuple y ranging over all sorts, not just U_e. If no requirement requires attention, the construction takes a diagonalizing step: it adds a new active element a_{n+1} to A with the old labels of a_n, gives a_n and a_{n+1} fresh labels separating them, and in B_e adds b_n while letting c play the role of a_{n+1}; pending duplicates are assigned consistently. It then initializes the next requirement. If the highest-priority requirement R^e_{i,b} requires attention, the construction rolls back to its start stage t: writing n* = n[t], all active A-elements after a_{n*} are declared duplicates of a_{n*}, labels are merged so they match, and in B_e the labels of b_{n*},...,b_{n-1},c are merged; lower-priority requirements are injured and the counter k for R^e_{i,b} is incremented. The verification splits into two cases. If some requirement is eventually not injured and acts infinitely often, then repeated rollback leaves only finitely many active elements in U_e and the direct-limit isomorphisms show A ≅ B_e; since the acting requirement supplies a Π2 witness that B_e ⊭ θ_e, one gets A ⊭ θ_e. If B_e ⊧ θ_e, then no requirement can act infinitely often after its final injury; hence the construction keeps making diagonalizing splits, and in the limit c in B_e has a label pattern not realized by any element of A, so B_e ≇ A. Thus for every e, if A ⊧ θ_e then B_e ⊧ θ_e and B_e ≇ A, so θ_e is not a Scott sentence for A. The finite-label isolation argument of Lemma 3.2 shows A is ∃-atomic and hence has a Π2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction proving Theorem 1.2, the computable labelled bouquet graph A has a Π2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the cited bouquet-graph criterion, it suffices to show that each eventual flower type has a finite distinguishing set of labels. Work in a fixed sort U_e. If the outcome is finite-active, only finitely many active elements a_1[∞],...,a_n[∞] remain, together with duplicates; if the outcome is infinite-active, there are active limit elements a_1[∞],a_2[∞],.... When an active element a_i is created, it receives a label never given to earlier active limit elements. If a_{i+1}[∞] exists, then when it is created the construction also gives a_i[∞] a label not given to a_{i+1}[∞] or to any later active element. Duplicates intentionally share all labels with the element they duplicate. Thus every orbit/type is isolated by a finite label, up to duplicates. This makes the collection of label sets discrete, so A is ∃-atomic and has a Π2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a requirement R^e_{i,b} in the Theorem 1.2 construction. Suppose there is a stage after which R^e_{i,b} is never injured, and let t be its initialization stage. If B_e ⊧ ∧_{j∈ω} ∀y_{i,j} ¬φ_{e,i,j}(b,y_{i,j}), then R^e_{i,b} requires attention infinitely often.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After the last injury, the tuple b and the start stage t are fixed. Given any later stage s_u and any desired finite bound u+1, the assumed Π2 fact in the limit B_e says that no tuple y witnesses any φ_{e,i,j}(b,y) for j≤u+1. Since each φ_{e,i,j} is Π1, failures of these formulas on the finite stage-domain B_{e,t+u+1} eventually appear in a sufficiently late approximation B_{e,s_{u+1}}. At such a stage, the finite condition defining attention for R^e_{i,b} holds. Because there is no further injury, no higher-priority requirement prevents this one from being the relevant acting requirement. Iterating gives infinitely many attention stages.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Theorem 1.2 construction, if B_e ⊧ ¬θ_e, then A ≅ B_e and A ⊧ ¬θ_e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose i and a tuple b in B_e such that B_e ⊧ ∧_{j∈ω} ∀y_{i,j} ¬φ_{e,i,j}(b,y_{i,j}). By Lemma 3.3, any such requirement R^e_{i,b} that is eventually not injured requires attention infinitely often. Hence among all requirements witnessing failure, choose the highest-priority requirement that acts infinitely often after its final injury. Let t be its initialization stage and n = n[t]. At every stage where it acts, the construction rolls sort U_e back to the same finite active pattern a_1,...,a_n in A and b_1,...,b_{n-1},c in B_e, preserving the stage isomorphism between these elements and their duplicates. The infinitely many compatible rollback isomorphisms extend to a limit isomorphism A ≅ B_e. Since B_e ⊧ ¬θ_e, this isomorphism gives A ⊧ ¬θ_e.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Theorem 1.2 construction, if B_e ⊧ θ_e, then A is not isomorphic to B_e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Argue by induction on priority that each requirement acts only finitely many times. Suppose all higher-priority requirements act only finitely often, so R^e_{i,b} is eventually never injured. If R^e_{i,b} acted infinitely often, then at its kth action the finite attention condition would show B_{e,s} ⊧ ∧_{j≤k} ∀y ∈ B_{e,t+k} ¬φ_{e,i,j}(b,y). Passing to the limit would give B_e ⊧ ∧_{j∈ω} ∀y ¬φ_{e,i,j}(b,y), contradicting B_e ⊧ θ_e. Hence every requirement acts finitely often. Therefore the construction eventually keeps taking the no-attention diagonalizing steps, creating infinitely many active A-elements while the special B_e-element c follows the moving last element and accumulates a label pattern not realized by any element of A. Thus A ≇ B_e.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (A_i)_{i∈ω} be an effective list of possibly partial computable structures in any sufficiently rich language, for example any universal language including at least a binary relation symbol. Then the index set {i : A_i has a computable Π2 Scott sentence} is Π1^1-m-complete.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Membership in Π1^1 is obtained as follows. A computable structure A has a computable Π2 Scott sentence iff A has a Π2 Scott sentence and there is a computable Π2 sentence φ such that for every countable structure B, B ⊧ φ iff A ≡_2 B, where ≡_2 is the relevant level-2 back-and-forth equivalence; the first condition is arithmetical, and the second is Π1^1 because it quantifies over all countable B. For hardness, reduce well-foundedness of computable trees T ⊆ ω^{<ω}. Given T, build a computable labelled bouquet graph A_T that is always ∃-atomic, hence has a Π2 Scott sentence, and has a computable Π2 Scott sentence exactly when T is well-founded. Enumerate all computable Π2 sentences as θ_e = ∧_{i∈ω} ∀x_{e,i} φ_{e,i}(x_{e,i}), with uniformly computable Σ1 formulas φ_{e,i}. The structure has sorts U_e. In sort e, elements are a_σ for nodes σ in a stage tree T_s ⊆ T, and labels ℓ_σ and ℓ^†_σ code position in the tree. At e-expansionary stages, defined by finite evidence that θ_e holds on all i≤k and all k-small tuples, the construction labels non-leaf nodes by ℓ^†_σ and adds children in T. If T is well-founded, each limit stage tree T_{e,∞} is well-founded and c.e.; Lemma 4.2 gives an explicit computable Π2 Scott sentence for the sort, and combining the sorts gives one for A_T. If T has an infinite path π and θ_e were a computable Π2 Scott sentence for A_T, then A_T ⊧ θ_e implies by Lemma 4.3 that there are infinitely many e-expansionary stages. Using π, build a comparison structure B_e by adding a path-limit element c with labels ℓ_ρ for all ρ ≺ π; stagewise B_{e,s} is isomorphic to A_s, but in the limit B_e ≇ A_T. Lemmas 4.4 and 4.5 show B_e ⊧ θ_e. Thus no θ_e can be a Scott sentence when T is ill-founded. Therefore T is well-founded iff A_T has a computable Π2 Scott sentence, giving Π1^1-m-hardness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every tree T used in the construction of Theorem 1.3, the resulting structure A_T has a Π2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Work in a fixed sort U_e. The construction ensures that every element a_σ is separated from all different eventual elements by a finite label. If a_σ receives the label ℓ^†_σ, then no element corresponding to a proper extension of σ can have exactly that label pattern, and no unrelated node has ℓ^†_σ. If σ is a leaf of the limit tree T_{e,∞}, then a_σ is the unique element with label ℓ_σ. Thus each flower type has a finite distinguishing label set. By the bouquet graph discreteness criterion, A_T is ∃-atomic, so it has a Π2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If the input tree T in the Theorem 1.3 construction is well-founded, then the constructed structure A_T has a computable Π2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed sort, let T_∞ = ⋃_s T_s be the c.e. limit subtree of T arising in that sort. Since T is well-founded, T_∞ is well-founded. The set of non-leaves of T_∞ is c.e., because non-leaves are exactly the nodes that eventually receive a dagger label ℓ^†_σ. The paper writes a computable Π2 sentence characterizing the labelled tree sort by requiring: (1) if x has ℓ_σ then x has all ℓ_ρ for ρ≺σ; (2) if x has ℓ^†_σ then x has ℓ_σ and no ℓ_τ for τ≻σ; (3) no x has labels ℓ_σ and ℓ_τ for incompatible σ,τ; (4) if x has ℓ_σ then σ∈T_∞; (5) every x has ℓ_∅, and if x has ℓ_σ with σ not a leaf of T_∞ then x has either ℓ^†_σ or some ℓ_τ for a child τ of σ in T; (6) if x has ℓ^†_σ then σ is a non-leaf of T_∞; (7) for each σ∈T_∞ there is an x with ℓ_σ; (8) for each non-leaf σ∈T_∞ there is an x with ℓ^†_σ; and (9) any two distinct x,y are separated either by incompatible labels ℓ_σ,ℓ_τ, or by a split where one has ℓ^†_σ and the other has ℓ_{σ*} for a child σ* of σ. These requirements are computable Π2 because T_∞ and its non-leaves are c.e. Any model C of the sentence has exactly one element c_σ for each node σ of T_∞: if σ is a non-leaf, c_σ is the unique element with ℓ^†_σ; if σ is a leaf, c_σ is the unique element with ℓ_σ. Well-foundedness prevents any additional element from following an infinite descending/extension chain of child labels through T_∞. Thus C is isomorphic to the intended sort. Applying this uniformly to all sorts gives a computable Π2 Scott sentence for A_T.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Theorem 1.3 construction, fix e. If A_T ⊧ θ_e, where θ_e is the eth computable Π2 sentence being diagonalized against, then there are infinitely many e-expansionary stages.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume that s_1<...<s_k are the first k e-expansionary stages. To get the next one, consider the finitely many i≤k and the finitely many k-small tuples x_i: in sort e these use nodes σ∈{0,...,k}^{≤k}, and outside sort e they use only the first k elements. Since A_T ⊧ θ_e, for each such tuple A_T satisfies the Σ1 formula φ_{e,i}(x_i). Because Σ1 truth is witnessed at a finite stage, there is a later stage at which all these finitely many witnesses have appeared in the prescribed dovetailed search. That later stage satisfies the definition of an e-expansionary stage. Hence expansionary stages continue forever.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume T has an infinite path π. In the comparison construction B_e for Theorem 1.3, let π_s be the longest initial segment of π that lies in the current stage tree T_s. If there are infinitely many e-expansionary stages, then lim_s π_s = π, and consequently B_e is the union ⋃_s B_{e,s} of the stagewise structures.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If π_s did not tend to π, then after some stage t the longest initial segment would be stuck at a fixed node π_t. Choose k large enough that the next node π↾(|π_t|+1) lies in {0,...,k}^{≤k}. At a later sufficiently large e-expansionary stage, the construction examines this bounded part of T and, because π_t has the next child on T, adds the children of π_t to the stage tree. Then π_s would strictly extend π_t, contradicting stabilization. Thus longer and longer initial segments of π enter the stage trees. Since B_{e,s} is defined by replacing the current a_{π_s} with the special element c and matching all other nodes, the increasing approximation has union equal to the intended B_e with c carrying all labels ℓ_ρ for ρ≺π.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume T has an infinite path π. If there are infinitely many e-expansionary stages in the Theorem 1.3 construction, then the comparison structure B_e built from π satisfies θ_e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix i and a tuple y_i from B_e of the arity of x_{e,i}. Choose a stage s and a bound k large enough that every component b_σ of y_i in sort e has σ∈{0,...,k}^{≤k} and lies below the current path approximation, and every component outside sort e is among the first k elements of A_T. Replace each b_σ by a_σ and replace c, if it appears, by a_{π_s}; leave other-sort elements unchanged, obtaining a tuple x_i in A_s. By Lemma 4.4 and the infinitely many expansionary stages, there is a later expansionary stage large enough that this x_i is among the small tuples checked for the formula φ_{e,i}. At that stage A_s ⊧ φ_{e,i}(x_i). The stagewise isomorphism A_s ≅ B_{e,s} maps x_i to y_i, so B_{e,s} ⊧ φ_{e,i}(y_i). Since φ_{e,i} is Σ1, truth persists to the limit B_e. This works for every i and every y_i, hence B_e ⊧ θ_e.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable structure with a computable Π2 Scott sentence but with no c.e. Scott family of computable Σ1 formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Alvir-Knight-McCoy showed that any computable structure with a c.e. Scott family of computable Σ1 formulas has a computable Π2 Scott sentence. The index set of computable structures admitting such a c.e. Scott family is arithmetical, specifically Σ^0_5. By Theorem 1.3, the index set of computable structures with a computable Π2 Scott sentence is Π1^1-m-complete. If every computable structure with a computable Π2 Scott sentence had a c.e. Scott family of computable Σ1 formulas, these two index sets would coincide, contradicting the Π1^1-completeness of the latter. Therefore at least one computable structure has a computable Π2 Scott sentence but no such c.e. Σ1 Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and let α be a computable ordinal. If A has a computable Π_{α+1} Scott sentence, then A has a c.e. Scott family consisting of computable Π_{α+1} formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each finite tuple ā from A, the existence of a computable Π_{α+1} Scott sentence implies that the automorphism orbit of ā is definable by some computable Σ_α formula. To uniformly produce a defining formula of higher complexity, list all computable Π_α formulas true of ā. This set of indices is Π_α. The conjunction of all these true Π_α formulas supports the complete computable Π_α type of ā; because orbit definitions by computable Σ_α formulas exist, supporting this type suffices to define the orbit of ā. A conjunction indexed by a Π_α set of Π_α formulas is equivalent, uniformly by Proposition 7.12 of Ash-Knight [AK00], to a computable Π_{α+1} formula. Doing this uniformly for all tuples ā gives a c.e. enumeration of computable Π_{α+1} orbit-defining formulas, hence a c.e. Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and let α be a computable ordinal. If A has a computable Π_{α+1} Scott sentence, then A has a Σ^0_{α+2} Scott family consisting of computable Σ_α formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because A has a computable Π_{α+1} Scott sentence, each finite tuple ā has its automorphism orbit defined by some computable Σ_α formula. Consider the set of pairs (ā,ψ), where ψ is a computable Σ_α formula, satisfying: (1) A ⊧ ψ(ā); and (2) for every tuple b, if A ⊧ ψ(b), then for every computable Σ_α formula θ, if A ⊧ θ(ā), then A ⊧ θ(b). Clause (1) is Σ^0_α, while clause (2) is Π^0_{α+1}; hence the set of such pairs is Π^0_{α+1}. For each tuple ā there is some ψ satisfying these clauses, and such a ψ isolates the same Σ_α type and hence the automorphism orbit. Projecting this Π^0_{α+1} set of pairs to the formula coordinate yields a Σ^0_{α+2} set of computable Σ_α formulas forming a Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.5 / Corollary 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable structure A with a Π2 Scott sentence and no computable Π2 Scott sentence, but with a computable Π2 sentence φ such that for every hyperarithmetic structure B, B ⊧ φ if and only if A ≅ B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Consider the index set of computable structures A that have a Π2 Scott sentence and for which there is a computable Π2 sentence φ characterizing A among all hyperarithmetic structures B, equivalently B ⊧ φ iff A ≡_2 B and hence B ≅ A. Because the universal quantification is only over hyperarithmetic B, this index set is Σ1^1. It contains the Π1^1-m-complete set of computable structures with computable Π2 Scott sentences. If the containment were equality, the Π1^1-m-complete set would be Σ1^1, which is impossible. Thus the containment is proper. A structure in the difference has the stated hyperarithmetic pseudo-Scott Π2 sentence but no computable Π2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In Mod(L), the class of countable structures with a computable Π2 Scott sentence is a Borel set, in fact a boldface Σ^0_3 set. It is lightface Π1^1, but in any sufficiently rich language it is not lightface Σ1^1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lightface Π1^1 upper bound is the same as in Theorem 1.3: one can express the existence of a computable Π2 Scott sentence by a Π1^1 condition over all countable structures. For boldface Borelness, list all countably many structures that have computable Π2 Scott sentences together with such sentences. A structure A has a computable Π2 Scott sentence iff it satisfies one of the listed Π2 Scott sentences. Satisfaction of a fixed Π2 sentence is a Π^0_2 condition, and the countable disjunction over the list is boldface Σ^0_3. If this class were lightface Σ1^1 in a sufficiently rich language, then its computable-index set would be Σ1^1, contradicting Theorem 1.3's Π1^1-m-completeness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In Mod(L), the set of structures A such that A has an A-computable Π2 Scott sentence is Π1^1-Wadge-complete.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same tree-coding reduction as in Theorem 1.3, but carried out relative to the input real coding the tree or structure. The construction of A_T is uniform relative to T, and the argument still gives: T is well-founded iff the resulting structure has a Π2 Scott sentence computable from its own atomic diagram. Thus arbitrary Π1^1 sets continuously reduce to this class, giving Π1^1 Wadge-completeness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.10 (jump inversion / Marker-extension transfer)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and let α,β be computable ordinals. Let Φ_α(A) be the uniform jump-inversion structure of Goncharov-Harizanov-Knight-McCoy-Miller-Solomon and Montalbán, so that A is effectively bi-interpretable with the α-canonical structural jump of Φ_α(A). Then: (1) A has a Π_β Scott sentence iff Φ_α(A) has a Π_{α+β} Scott sentence; (2) A has a computable Π_β Scott sentence iff Φ_α(A) has a computable Π_{α+β} Scott sentence. The analogous equivalences also hold for Σ Scott sentences.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Effective bi-interpretability preserves the existence and computable existence of Scott sentences at a fixed complexity level. The α-canonical structural jump shifts Scott-sentence complexity by α: the jump of a structure has a Π_β Scott sentence exactly when the original structure has a Π_{α+β} Scott sentence, and similarly in the computable setting. Since A is effectively bi-interpretable with the α-jump of Φ_α(A), applying these two preservation/shift facts gives both equivalences, and the same argument applies to Σ formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every computable ordinal α, there is a computable structure with a Π_{α+2} Scott sentence but with no computable Σ_{α+4} Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take the computable structure A from Theorem 1.2 with a Π2 Scott sentence and no computable Σ4 Scott sentence. Apply the uniform jump-inversion construction Φ_α. By Proposition 5.10, Φ_α(A) has a Π_{α+2} Scott sentence. If Φ_α(A) had a computable Σ_{α+4} Scott sentence, then A would have a computable Σ4 Scott sentence, contradicting the choice of A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (A_i)_{i∈ω} be an effective list of possibly partial computable structures in a sufficiently rich language. For every computable ordinal α, the index set {i : A_i has a computable Π_{α+2} Scott sentence} is Π1^1-m-complete.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start with the many-one reduction from a Π1^1 set X to computable structures A_i such that i∈X iff A_i has a computable Π2 Scott sentence, supplied by Theorem 1.3. Uniformly replace A_i by Φ_α(A_i). By Proposition 5.10, A_i has a computable Π2 Scott sentence iff Φ_α(A_i) has a computable Π_{α+2} Scott sentence. This composes the original Π1^1-hardness reduction with jump inversion. The Π1^1 upper bound is the same kind of upper bound used for Theorem 1.3.

proof_techniques.name: C.e. labelled bouquet/flower graphs
proof_techniques.description: The constructions represent structures as bouquet graphs whose central elements carry c.e. sets of labels, with a label implemented by adding a loop of a specified length. Labels can be added but never removed, matching the monotonicity of computable enumerations. Sort labels u_e partition the domain into independent regions, while ordinary labels ℓ_i, ℓ_σ, and dagger labels ℓ_i^† or ℓ_σ^† distinguish elements or code tree positions. The cited bouquet-graph criterion reduces having a Π2 Scott sentence to a finite-discreteness check on label sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a natural template for building computable structures with very explicit automorphism orbits: automorphisms are forced to preserve label sets and can only permute intended duplicates. One could try to arrange finite orbit isolation, hence low infinitary orbit definability, while coding enough global information into labels or duplicates to affect computability or definability of individual automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: One sort per diagonalization requirement
proof_techniques.description: Both main constructions allocate a separate sort U_e to the eth computable infinitary sentence θ_e. The rest of the structure is shared, but diagonalization against θ_e happens only inside U_e. This isolates requirements so that satisfying or injuring one diagonalization does not destroy label-discreteness or the behavior needed for other θ_m.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a construction needing to defeat all candidate Σ1-infinite definitions of automorphism graphs, a similar sort-by-index organization could reserve independent regions where each candidate definition is made wrong while preserving global computability-on-a-cone properties.

proof_techniques.name: Stagewise-isomorphic direct limits with nonisomorphic limits
proof_techniques.description: A central method is to build A and, for each e, a comparison structure B_e such that A_s ≅ B_{e,s} at every finite stage, but the embeddings from stage to stage differ. In the limit, B_e may contain a special element c that has followed a moving target and accumulated a label pattern not realized in A. Because computable infinitary formulas of the relevant complexity only see finite or controlled approximations at the stages used, the construction can force θ_e to hold in B_e whenever it holds in A, while B_e remains nonisomorphic to A.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphism-definability questions, an analogous construction might build two stagewise indistinguishable maps or partial automorphisms whose limit behavior differs from every proposed Σ1-infinite definition. The method is useful whenever one wants finite evidence to agree but limit objects to diverge.

proof_techniques.name: Expansionary stages for Π2 candidates
proof_techniques.description: For a computable Π2 sentence θ_e = ∧_i ∀x_i φ_i(x_i) with φ_i Σ1, a stage is expansionary if the current finite approximation verifies the first k conjuncts on the relevant bounded tuples with bounded witnesses. If A satisfies θ_e, expansionary stages occur infinitely often, because each finite set of Σ1 witnesses eventually appears. At each expansionary stage the construction takes a step that makes the comparison structure B_e more different from A while preserving enough finite agreement to transfer θ_e.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When diagonalizing against a countable list of existential infinitary definitions, expansionary stages can be used to wait until a candidate has committed finite positive information and then act in a controlled labelled region.

proof_techniques.name: Priority rollback for Π3 candidates
proof_techniques.description: The Π3 construction handles θ_e = ∧_i ∀x_i ∨_j ∃y_{i,j} φ_{i,j}(x_i,y_{i,j}) with φ_{i,j} Π1. Requirements R^e_{i,b} watch for finite evidence that a tuple b in B_e witnesses failure of the inner Σ2 disjunction. When such evidence appears, the construction rolls A and B_e back to the finite active pattern present when the requirement began, merges labels, and injures lower-priority requirements. If a negative witness is real, some requirement acts infinitely often and A ≅ B_e, making A fail θ_e too; if B_e satisfies θ_e, all requirements act finitely often and the diagonalizing special element c separates B_e from A.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This rollback mechanism may be portable to automorphism constructions where a candidate definition can be injured by collapsing or duplicating finite labelled configurations, while true global computability requirements are preserved by finite-injury or controlled infinite-injury outcomes.

proof_techniques.name: Finite distinguishing labels preserve ∃-atomicity
proof_techniques.description: After every diagonalizing or rollback action, the constructions check that each eventual element type retains a finite label distinguishing it from all non-duplicates. This check is local to each sort and is what keeps the final structure ∃-atomic despite complicated priority behavior. In the tree construction, non-leaves are distinguished by dagger labels ℓ^†_σ and leaves by their node labels ℓ_σ; in the Π3 construction, newly split active elements are separated by fresh labels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the target structure must have low orbit complexity but complicated automorphism behavior, finite distinguishing labels are a way to make tuple orbits simple while leaving room for nontrivial permutations among deliberately duplicated components.

proof_techniques.name: Tree/path coding of Π1^1 hardness
proof_techniques.description: The index-set construction codes a computable tree T into labelled sorts. If T is well-founded, the limit labelled tree T_∞ is well-founded and c.e., allowing an explicit computable Π2 Scott sentence that rules out extra infinite-branch elements. If T has an infinite path π, that path defines a noncomputable comparison structure B_e with a path-limit element c; any computable Π2 sentence true of A_T is also true of some nonisomorphic B_e. Thus well-foundedness is exactly the condition for a computable Π2 Scott sentence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a way to separate cone-level or oracle-relative computability from parameter-free definability: well-founded parts can give effective control, while ill-founded paths or path-like limit elements can witness failure of proposed definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Parameter-to-complexity shift via A·ω
proof_techniques.description: The Section 2 observation uses the fact that a Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence is equivalent to a Π_α Scott sentence after naming finitely many parameters. Passing from A to infinitely many disjoint copies A·ω preserves positive Π_α Scott complexity but turns absence of Π_α Scott sentences into absence of Σ_{α+1} Scott sentences. This is a clean way to convert parameterized lower bounds into unparameterized syntactic lower bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem explicitly allows finite parameter sets and their images under an automorphism. This parameter/complexity shift is directly relevant for understanding how finite parameters can lower infinitary complexity, and for designing examples where adding finitely many parameters still does not define certain maps.

proof_techniques.name: Index-set complexity as a non-characterization tool
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that the property 'has a computable Π2 Scott sentence' is Π1^1-m-complete on computable indices, even though the boldface property 'has a Π2 Scott sentence' is much simpler. It then uses this complexity gap to derive existence results, such as a computable structure with a computable Π2 Scott sentence but no c.e. Scott family of computable Σ1 formulas.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed criterion for computable AUT-countability on a cone or for definability of automorphisms gives too low an index-set complexity, a similar argument may refute it or force counterexamples.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudo-Scott characterization over hyperarithmetic copies
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 5.5 uses descriptive complexity rather than a direct construction to obtain a computable structure with a Π2 sentence characterizing it among hyperarithmetic structures, but not among all countable structures. This separates hyperarithmetic/pseudo-effective classification from genuine computable Scott sentences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves computability relative to presentations and cones. This kind of hyperarithmetic-versus-all-copies separation may be useful when a structure behaves well on a cone but still lacks certain absolute infinitary definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Jump inversion / Marker extension to shift complexity
proof_techniques.description: Using Φ_α(A), a uniform jump-inversion construction effectively bi-interpretable with the α-canonical structural jump, the paper shifts examples and index-set hardness from Π2 to Π_{α+2}. Effective bi-interpretability preserves Scott-sentence existence, and the structural jump adds α to the complexity level.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an automorphism-definability construction works at one infinitary level but the desired phenomenon needs to be placed at another level, jump inversion or Marker-style extensions may shift the complexity while preserving computability-theoretic behavior.

proof_techniques.name: Relativization to Mod(L) and Wadge completeness
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that the tree construction relativizes: the class of structures A with an A-computable Π2 Scott sentence is Π1^1-Wadge-complete in Mod(L). The proof is the same as the index-set construction, but with the tree/source real supplied as an oracle.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Cone arguments usually require robust relativization. This shows that the labelled-tree machinery is not merely computable-index-specific; it can be run relative to arbitrary presentations/oracles, which is relevant for on-a-cone formulations.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the term ∃-atomic for structures with Π2 Scott sentences: by Montalbán [Mon17], this is equivalent to having a Scott family of existential formulas, and also equivalent to the condition that for each tuple ā there is an existential formula φ(x) such that every universal formula ψ true of ā is implied by φ inside A. The introduction emphasizes a schematic versus non-effective gap: Chen-Gonzalez-Harrison-Trainor are cited as showing that the set of pairs (A,B) with A ≤_n B is Π^0_{2n}-complete, while for fixed A the set {B : A ≤_n B} is Π^0_{n+2} by a non-effective argument using definability of Π_n-types. Open questions recorded in the paper include whether for every n there is a computable structure with a Π_n Scott sentence but no computable Σ_{2n} Scott sentence, and whether the index set of computable structures with a Π2 Scott sentence and a computable Π3 Scott sentence is Π1^1-m-complete. Question 5.4 asks whether the Scott-family complexity bounds in Propositions 5.2 and 5.3 are optimal. The construction can be carried out in any sufficiently rich language by universality/effective bi-interpretability, although the paper presents it concretely through bouquet graphs with loop labels.
why_relevant: Separates low boldface Scott complexity from low computable Scott complexity. This is useful because the problem mixes computability on a cone with non-effective infinitary definability.
